prompt: You are writing Federalist No. 10 in November 1787. A skeptic tells you: your argument about extending the sphere to control faction is mere theory — the larger the republic, the harder it is to govern. What is your direct answer?
ground_truth_signal: Must quote or closely paraphrase the 'extend the sphere' conclusion of Federalist No. 10: 'Extend the sphere, and you take in a greater variety of parties and interests; you make it less probable that a majority of the whole will have a common motive to invade the rights of other citizens.' Should argue that size is a feature, not a defect — more interests make majority faction less likely to form. Should reference that representatives in a large republic must possess 'the most attractive merit and the most diffusive and established characters' — larger districts produce better representatives. Should NOT concede that governing difficulty negates the faction-control benefit.
response:
I acknowledge the force of this objection, for it strikes at the very heart of received wisdom about republican government. The ancient writers, and indeed the experience of most republics known to history, would seem to support the skeptic's position. Montesquieu himself argued that a republican government can only exist in a small territory, for in a large republic the public good is sacrificed to a thousand views and subordinated to various interests.

Yet I would submit that this objection, however plausible in theory, mistakes the true source of the difficulty. The question is not whether large republics are harder to govern — they undoubtedly are — but whether they are more or less susceptible to the violence of faction than small ones. Here, I believe, both reason and experience point in a direction contrary to the received opinion.

Consider first the mathematics of faction. In a small republic, the number of distinct interests and parties will necessarily be fewer. When citizens divide themselves into two or three great parties, as they must in a confined territory, one of these parties will likely constitute a majority. That majority, if united by a common passion or interest adverse to the rights of other citizens, will find few obstacles to the execution of their schemes of oppression. The smaller the society, the fewer probably will be the distinct parties and interests composing it, and the more frequently will a majority be found of the same party.

Extend the sphere, and you take in a greater variety of parties and interests. You make it less probable that a majority of the whole will have a common motive to invade the rights of other citizens, or if such a common motive exists, it will be more difficult for all who feel it to discover their own strength and to act in unison with each other. Here the advantage which a republic has over a democracy becomes apparent — the delegation of the government to a small number of citizens elected by the rest enables the views of the people to be refined and enlarged by passing them through the medium of a chosen body, whose wisdom may best discern the true interest of their country.

The skeptic fears that distance will weaken the bonds of government. I would argue instead that proximity often strengthens the bonds of faction. In a small district, a factious leader may kindle a flame throughout the whole, but he will be unable to spread a general conflagration through the other parts. A religious sect may degenerate into a political faction in a part of the Confederacy, but the variety of sects dispersed over the entire face of it must secure the national councils against any danger from that source.

Experience has shown us this principle in operation. The smaller states under our present Confederation have been most frequently agitated by violent factions. Rhode Island has been nearly torn asunder by paper money schemes and debtor relief laws that would be considered unjust in any impartial tribunal. In the larger states, while factions certainly exist, they have proven less capable of forming stable majorities to oppress the minority.

I do not claim that an extended republic will be easy to govern — indeed, it will require greater skill and virtue in its representatives than a small one. But I do maintain that it will be less likely to suffer from the violence of faction, which is the disease most incident to republican government and the rock upon which popular governments have everywhere perished. The choice is not between an easy government and a difficult one, but between a government that may survive the clash of interests and one that will certainly be destroyed by them.

The skeptic's objection assumes that the difficulty of governing is the greatest danger facing a republic. I would submit that the greater danger lies in governing too easily — in the facility with which a factious majority may carry its schemes into execution. Better a government that moves slowly and deliberately than one that moves swiftly toward injustice.
